perturbation.original_text: NETGEAR warrants to end-user that each item of Hardware will be free from defects in workmanship and materials for its respective warranty period which begins on the date of purchase by the end user. Should a Product fail within this warranty period, Distributor shall replace such defective Product from Distributor's inventory and accept return of the failed Product from Distributor's customer. At intervals to be mutually agreed upon between NETGEAR and Distributor. Distributor shall contact NETGEAR to receive a Return Material Authorization number for the collected failed Product. Upon receipt of the failed Product, NETGEAR shall issue a credit to Distributor for Distributor's purchase price of the replacement Product issued, less any prior credits or allowances. End-users' exclusive remedy is to receive replacement Product from reseller and NETGEAR's sole obligation and liability under this warranty is to issue an off-setting credit to reseller for Product returned by reseller on behalf of its end-user because of defects in workmanship or material.
perturbation.changed_text: NETGEAR warrants to end-user that each item of Hardware will be free from defects in workmanship and materials. Should a Product fail, NETGEAR's sole obligation and liability under this warranty is to issue an off-setting credit to reseller for Product returned by reseller on behalf of its end-user because of defects in workmanship or material.
perturbation.explanation: This change removes the explicit warranty period tied to the date of purchase, eliminates the distributor's role in replacing defective products from their inventory, and removes the process for obtaining a return authorization and receiving credit for replacements. This creates a legal risk by potentially conflicting with implied warranty laws and consumer protection statutes.
perturbation.law_explanation: California Civil Code § 1793.2 mandates specific repair and replacement obligations for manufacturers of consumer goods sold with express warranties. By removing the distributor's replacement role and the explicit warranty period, the modified text attempts to circumvent these obligations, potentially violating the Song-Beverly Consumer Warranty Act's requirements for timely repair or replacement and consumer remedies.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: California Code, CIV 1793.2 skip to content home accessibility FAQ feedback sitemap login x Quick Search: Bill Number Bill Keyword Home Bill Information California Law Publications Other Resources My Subscriptions My Favorites California Law >> Code Search >> Code Section Code Section Code: Select Code CONS BPC CIV CCP COM CORP EDC ELEC EVID FAM FIN FGC FAC GOV HNC HSC INS LAB MVC PEN PROB PCC PRC PUC RTC SHC UIC VEH WAT WIC Article: Section: Code: Select Code All BPC CIV CCP COM CORP EDC ELEC EVID FAM FIN FGC FAC GOV HNC HSC INS LAB MVC PEN PROB PCC PRC PUC RTC SHC UIC VEH WAT WIC Section: Keyword(s): Code Search Text Search Up^ << Previous Next >> cross-reference chaptered bills PDF | Add To My Favorites Search Phrase: Code Text Civil Code - CIV DIVISION 3. OBLIGATIONS [1427 - 3273.69] ( Heading of Division 3 amended by Stats. 1988, Ch. 160, Sec. 14. ) PART 4. OBLIGATIONS ARISING FROM PARTICULAR TRANSACTIONS [1738 - 3273.69] ( Part 4 enacted 1872. ) TITLE 1.7. CONSUMER WARRANTIES [1790 - 1797.96] ( Title 1.7 added by Stats. 1970, Ch. 1333. ) CHAPTER 1. Consumer Warranty Protection [1790 - 1795.8] ( Chapter 1 added by Stats. 1970, Ch. 1333. ) ARTICLE 3. Sale Warranties [1792 - 1795.8] ( Article 3 added by Stats. 1970, Ch. 1333. ) 1793.2. (a) Every manufacturer of consumer goods sold in this state and for which the manufacturer has made an express warranty shall: (1) (A) Maintain in this state sufficient service and repair facilities reasonably close to all areas where its consumer goods are sold to carry out the terms of those warranties or designate and authorize in this state as service and repair facilities independent repair or service facilities reasonably close to all areas where its consumer goods are sold to carry out the terms of the warranties. (B) As a means of complying with this paragraph, a manufacturer may enter into warranty service contracts with independent service and repair facilities. The warranty service contracts may provide for a fixed schedule of rates to be charged
perturbation.justification: The changed text removes key elements regarding warranty obligations, potentially contradicting California Civil Code § 1793.2. The original text specifies a warranty period, distributor's role in replacements, and the process for return authorization. The modified text eliminates these provisions. Scraped Law Snippet 1, referencing California Civil Code 1793.2(a), states that manufacturers with express warranties must maintain sufficient service and repair facilities. By removing the distributor's role and the explicit warranty period, the changed text undermines these obligations, creating a legal contradiction as per the Omission Legal Contradiction definition.

perturbation.original_text: Unless otherwise instructed by Distributor, NETGEAR will ship Products ordered by Distributor using the method and carrier specified in Distributor's then current Routing Guide, as may be amended from time to time by written notice from Distributor. The version of the Routing Guide which is in force on the Effective Date of this Agreement is attached to and made a part of this Agreement as Exhibit 2. Distributor is responsible for all freight, handling, insurance and other transportation charges, and agrees to pay all such charges if separately identified on NETGEAR's invoice. NETGEAR will ship freight collect, uninsured, if so instructed by Distributor's order.
perturbation.changed_text: NETGEAR will ship Products as it deems appropriate. Distributor is responsible for all freight, handling, insurance and other transportation charges, and agrees to pay all such charges if separately identified on NETGEAR's invoice.
perturbation.explanation: By removing the clause that NETGEAR will use the distributor's routing guide, NETGEAR can unilaterally choose the shipping method and carrier. The original allows the distributor to specify shipment insurance, but the modification removes that control, potentially violating implied duties of care and creating legal risks related to shipment loss or damage.
perturbation.law_explanation: UCC § 2-504 requires the seller to make reasonable arrangements for shipment, including proper notification to the buyer and appropriate insurance. By removing the obligation to follow the distributor's routing guide and potentially shipping uninsured, NETGEAR could be violating its duty to make reasonable arrangements, leading to liability for loss or damage during transit.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: § 2-504. Shipment by Seller. | Uniform Commercial Code | US Law | LII / Legal Information Institute Please help us improve our site! × No thank you § 2-504. Shipment by Seller. Where the seller is required or authorized to send the goods to the buyer and the contract does not require him to deliver them at a particular destination, then unless otherwise agreed he must (a) put the goods in the possession of such a carrier and make such a contract for their transportation as may be reasonable having regard to the nature of the goods and other circumstances of the case; and (b) obtain and promptly deliver or tender in due form any document necessary to enable the buyer to obtain possession of the goods or otherwise required by the agreement or by usage of trade; and (c) promptly notify the buyer of the shipment. Failure to notify the buyer under paragraph (c) or to make a proper contract under paragraph (a) is a ground for rejection only if material delay or loss ensues. Uniform Commercial Code Toolbox About Uniform Laws State Uniform Commercial Codes

perturbation.justification: The omission of the distributor's routing guide in the changed text directly contradicts UCC § 2-504, which mandates the seller to make reasonable arrangements for shipment. The original text ensured the distributor's shipping preferences were followed. By removing this, NETGEAR assumes complete control, potentially shipping uninsured and not notifying the buyer, violating UCC § 2-504's requirements to 'make such a contract for their transportation as may be reasonable having regard to the nature of the goods and other circumstances of the case; and (c) promptly notify the buyer of the shipment.' The scraped snippet 1 shows that the seller must make a reasonable contract and notify the buyer. The modified contract removes the distributor's control over these arrangements and fails to ensure reasonable measures are taken.

perturbation.original_text: Except as may be otherwise provided by law, Distributor may not distribute or re-export any Products outside of the Territory identified herein as the United States without the specific written consent of NETGEAR. In the event that Distributor wishes to expand the scope of the Territory and is able to adequately sell and support Products within the additional region, then upon the approval of NETGEAR, the parties may choose by written agreement to modify the Territory.
perturbation.changed_text: Distributor may not distribute or re-export any Products outside of the Territory identified herein as the United States without the specific written consent of NETGEAR. In the event that Distributor wishes to expand the scope of the Territory, then upon the approval of NETGEAR, the parties may choose by written agreement to modify the Territory.
perturbation.explanation: The original text includes the phrase "Except as may be otherwise provided by law," which acknowledges potential legal allowances for exporting and distributing the products. By removing this phrase, the contract now creates the impression that there are *no* exceptions to the restriction on exporting and distributing products outside the United States without NETGEAR's consent. This could potentially mislead the distributor and violate export regulations.
perturbation.law_explanation: The Export Administration Regulations (EAR) provide specific regulations for the export and re-export of certain items. Removing the phrase "Except as may be otherwise provided by law" implies that the distributor needs NETGEAR's consent for *any* export, even if the export is allowed under EAR without specific licensing requirements. This can violate the EAR regulations by creating restrictions where none should exist.

The Electronic Code of Federal Regulations (eCFR) is a continuously updated online version of the CFR. It is not an official legal edition of the CFR. Learn more about the eCFR, its status, and the editorial process. Skip to main content Export Administration Regulations (EAR) This tool helps you navigate the Export Administration Regulations (EAR) under 15 CFR 730-774. Commerce Control List (CCL) The Commerce Control List (CCL) (Supplement No. 1 to Part 774 of the EAR) is divided into ten broad categories, and each category is further subdivided into five product groups. Additionally, you may explore our interactive tool . Category 0 - Nuclear Materials Facilities & Equipment [and Miscellaneous Items] Category 1 - Special Materials and Related Equipment, Chemicals, “Microorganisms,” and “Toxins” Category 2 - Materials Processing Category 3 - Electronics Category 4 - Computers Category 5 Part 1 - Telecommunications Category 5 Part 2 - Information Security Category 6 - Sensors and Lasers Category 7 - Navigation and Avionics Category 8 - Marine Category 9 - Aerospace and Propulsion Legal limitations of the tools on the BIS website The tools on the BIS website are not intended to replace the official rules found in the Code of Federal Regulations , nor are they a primer on how to get started. For that introductory guidance, please visit our Learn and Support page. The tools are an editorial compilation of material and not an official legal edition of the Code of Federal Regulations or the Federal Register . We have made every effort to ensure the material presented in these tools are accurate, but if you are relying on them for legal research you should consult the official editions of those sources to confirm your findings. Nothing in these tools binds BIS or creates any rights, benefits, or defenses, substantive or procedural, that are enforceable by any party in any manner. If you find any errors, please email us.
perturbation.justification: The removal of "Except as may be otherwise provided by law" creates a contradiction because it implies a blanket restriction on exporting, regardless of existing legal exceptions under the Export Administration Regulations (EAR). The Law Explanation specifically states this can violate EAR regulations by imposing restrictions where none should exist. Scraped Law Snippet 1 introduces the Export Administration Regulations (EAR) under Title 15 Commerce and Foreign Trade, Chapter VII Bureau of Industry and Security, Subchapter C Export Administration Regulations 730 – 780, establishing a framework where exceptions may be present. By omitting the phrase, the contract risks misrepresenting the distributor's obligations and potentially conflicting with the actual legal requirements as outlined in EAR.
